home.headline: I build AI systems that ship.
home.lede: Most AI projects die in the demo. I take them the rest of the way — through the frontend, the backend, the pipeline, and the boring parts nobody demos.
home.capabilitiesTitle: Four disciplines, one person to brief.
home.ossTeaserTitle: Good code doesn't mind an audience.
home.ossTeaserBody: Two projects of my own across five published packages, plus fixes merged into repos I don't own — immer, typedash, and Pane, a manager for running AI coding agents in parallel. Good company to keep.
home.writingBody: I learn it twice — once building it, once explaining it. Architecture, migrations, and the real cost of tech debt.
home.certsTeaserTitle: A second opinion, not a self-assessment.
home.certsTeaserBody: Thirty-three exams graded by someone other than me — the three major clouds, MongoDB, Python, Git and GitHub, and a stack of Scrum. Anyone can claim a skill; not everyone volunteers to be tested on it.
home.closingBody: Bring the part that's stuck. Twenty minutes, no pitch.
headlinePhrases: I build AI systems that ship.
I make agents do real work.
I own it end to end: FE, BE, CI.
I automate what nobody wants to.

ai.title: AI
ai.body: Agents, tool use, evals and the glue that makes a model useful in production instead of impressive in a notebook.

frontend.title: Frontend
frontend.body: React and TypeScript at scale — the architecture that keeps a codebase fast to work in three years from now.

backend.title: Backend
backend.body: APIs, data modelling and the services underneath. Certified across AWS, Azure, GCP and MongoDB.

automation.title: CI & Automation
automation.body: Pipelines, releases and the tedious work that should have been a script. GitHub Actions, all the way down.

oss.title: Open Source
oss.lede: The best code I've read was open, free, and maintained by people who owed me nothing. I learned the craft from those repos, so I try to leave them better than I found them — as an author, as a maintainer, and as a guest in codebases that aren't mine. Code read by strangers gets held to a higher bar, and engineers who write good code are rarely afraid to show it.
oss.authoredBody: Two projects I built and maintain under the rxova org, shipping five published packages.
oss.contributedBody: Fixes merged into codebases I don't own, where the bar is someone else's and the reviewer owes me nothing. That's the most useful review there is.

pane.blurb: Terminal-first, agent-agnostic manager for running multiple AI coding agents in parallel — locally or self-hosted.
pane.pr320: Added keyboard navigation to dropdown menus
pane.pr301: Fixed a node-pty crash on Intel Macs in the universal build

journey.blurb: A tiny stepper, wizard and sequencer with zero runtime dependencies.

useEverywhere.blurb: React hooks for state and messages shared across tabs, windows and workers.

typedash.blurb: A modern, type-safe alternative to lodash.
typedash.pr174: Guarded chunk() against a size below one

pie.blurb: Just Eat Takeaway's design system — the web components behind their apps.
pie.pr2458: Stopped the checkbox tick animating on mount, so a pre-checked box no longer flickers

immer.blurb: The immutable state library behind Redux Toolkit.
immer.pr1269: Return the real constructor on drafts, fixing a lodash.isEmpty crash
immer.issue1268: Reported the regression, with a minimal reproduction

writing.title: Blog Posts
writing.lede: Writing is how I find out whether I actually understood something — the essay always finds the hole the working code managed to hide. So I publish what I learn on Medium: architecture, migrations, TypeScript, and why zero tech debt is a myth. Someone else's post got me unstuck more than once. This is me paying it back.
writing.feedNote: The 10 most recent, straight from the feed.

certifications.title: Certifications
certifications.lede: Anyone can say they know a thing. Not everyone volunteers to be tested on it. These are 33 exams graded by someone other than me — thirteen from Scrum.org, the three major clouds, MongoDB, Python, Git and GitHub. A certificate doesn't make anyone a good engineer. But I'd rather hand you a second opinion than a self-assessment.

contact.title: Let's talk
contact.lede: Tell me what you're working on. Twenty minutes, no slides — bring the thing that's stuck, the architecture you're unsure about, or the plan that keeps slipping. If I can help, I'll show you how. If I can't, I'll usually know who can.
contact.formNote: I read everything and reply within a couple of days.

notFound.title: Nothing here.
notFound.body: That page doesn't exist — or it did, and the redesign ate it.
